title: How a 15-person marketing agency cut client reporting from 8 hours to 45 minutes with Claude
summary: A boutique marketing agency serving 22 clients replaced a 3-person reporting function with a Claude-based workflow — saving 143 hours/month and $8,200/month in labor — without losing a single client in the transition.
case_study:
Meridian Creative (name changed) is a 15-person performance marketing agency in Austin, TX. Monthly retainer revenue: $340K. Client count: 22. Their problem was one every agency owner recognizes: the monthly report was killing them.

Each client received a custom PDF report covering Google Ads performance, Meta results, SEO movement, and content metrics. A senior account manager spent roughly 2 hours per client pulling data from four platforms, drafting narrative commentary, and formatting the document. With 22 clients and a 4-person account team rotating the work, reporting consumed 44 hours every month — nearly a full week of a senior hire's time — before any actual client conversations happened.

They had tried Looker Studio. The templates were rigid, the commentary was absent, and clients kept asking for the 'story' anyway. They tried delegating to a VA. Data accuracy issues resulted in a client threatening to cancel. They were stuck.

The solution: a Python script that pulls raw data from Google Ads API, Meta Ads API, Google Analytics 4, and Ahrefs — into a structured JSON payload — then passes it to Claude via the API with a carefully engineered system prompt containing: the agency's house voice, each client's specific goals and benchmarks, any month-specific context the account manager adds in 3 minutes, and output formatting instructions for a Notion template.

Claude generates the full narrative commentary: trend analysis, anomaly explanations, strategic recommendations, and an executive summary. The account manager reviews, tweaks maybe 2 sentences, exports to PDF. Total time per client: 45 minutes, down from 2 hours. Total monthly time recovered: 27 hours.
implementation_guide:
Step 1 — Map your data sources. List every platform you pull data from. Each needs an API connection (or a CSV export process as fallback). Budget 2-4 hours per data source for the integration work.

Step 2 — Build the data aggregation script. Use Python with the official API SDKs for each platform. Structure the output as a single JSON object with consistent keys: client_id, reporting_period, channel_data, goals, prior_period_data. This structured payload is what you'll pass to Claude.

Step 3 — Engineer the system prompt. This is where most teams fail. Your system prompt needs: (a) the agency voice and tone guidelines — specific, with examples; (b) the client's goals in plain language; (c) strict instructions to use ONLY data from the payload — no hallucinated numbers; (d) instructions for a 'DATA UNAVAILABLE' placeholder when a metric is missing; (e) the exact output structure you want.

Step 4 — Build the Notion/Google Docs template. Define the sections, formatting, and placeholder tokens that Claude's output will populate.

Step 5 — Human review checkpoint. Non-negotiable: a senior team member reads every report before it goes to the client. The time savings are in generation, not review elimination.

Step 6 — Test on 3 clients before rolling out. Compare AI-generated reports to manually written ones. Have account managers review both blind.

Step 7 — Iterate the system prompt based on feedback from your first 30 days. Expect to revise it 4-6 times before it's stable.

numbers.wait_the_original_said_8200: Note: The $8,200/month figure in the summary reflects the 3 FTE account manager hours including benefits/overhead at fully-loaded cost of $85/hr equivalent

tool_stack.use: Data aggregation from 4 APIs into structured JSON
Report template population and PDF export
Monthly trigger, Slack notification when report ready for review

what_failed:
Attempt 1 — Generic prompt: They gave Claude raw CSV data with a simple instruction: 'write a marketing performance report.' The output was technically accurate but read like it was written by someone who had never spoken to a client. No strategic voice. No judgment. Clients noticed immediately and one asked if something had changed.

Attempt 2 — Over-engineering the prompt: They overcorrected by stuffing 4,000 words of brand guidelines, historical context, and client background into the system prompt. Claude started hallucinating historical data points that weren't in the payload — fabricating campaign metrics from prior months it had no data on. They nearly sent a report with invented numbers. This near-miss became their most important lesson.

What worked: A disciplined 800-word system prompt with three non-negotiable rules: (1) only use data present in the JSON payload, (2) output 'DATA NOT AVAILABLE FOR THIS PERIOD' when a metric is missing, (3) structure output as JSON first so the Notion integration is clean. Human review is the last gate — not optional.
takeaway: The leverage in AI-assisted reporting isn't the generation — it's the data architecture. Agencies that win with this spend 70% of their implementation time on clean, structured data pipelines and 30% on prompting. Agencies that fail do it backwards. The system prompt is the last problem to solve, not the first.
